quote: It's not really for us, the economists, to choose between axioms. Our job is to identify those combinations of axioms that are compatible and those that aren't; if possible, when axioms are parameterized, to describe the trade-offs between them in a quantitative way. That's part of what the axiomatic program contributes. But it's up to the users of our services — the bankruptcy judge, the relief agency, the housing office of a university, the scheduling office of a hospital, the administrator of a school district or student exchange program, the tax authority, the immigration office, and so on, and ultimately, the recipients of the goods and services — to decide which combination of axioms best captures what matters to them the most.

stance.implicit_norm: the economist's job is to MAP the trade-off, not to CHOOSE the point
stance.trigger: prescriptive question 'which axiom should we use?'
stance.verdict_pattern: role separation: economist = mapper, user = chooser
stance.rhetorical_device: cumulative list of users (judge, agency, university, hospital, etc.) — the multiplicity is the argument: there are many users, no single axiom can serve all

applicability.use_when: deciding whether to recommend an axiom, OR when finalizing a candidate axiom in v0.2
applicability.use_how: always anchor a new axiom to: (1) a quantitative trade-off curve, (2) a specific planner's problem

process_meta.[HUNCH]: 从 Thomson 风格抽出的 tacit judgment
